q: The remains of a Viking settlement in Canada, now a World Heritage site, are located at which place?
expl: The Vikings from Iceland who colonized Greenland 1,000 years ago also reached Labrador and the island of Newfoundland. The remains of their settlement, l'Anse aux Meadows, are a World Heritage site.

q: Who, in 1497, was the first to draw a map of Canada's East Coast?
expl: European exploration began in earnest in 1497 with the expedition of John Cabot, who was the first to draw a map of Canada's East Coast.

q: Between 1534 and 1542, Jacques Cartier made three voyages across the Atlantic, claiming the land for whom?
expl: Jacques Cartier made three voyages between 1534 and 1542, claiming the land for King Francis I of France.

q: The name 'Canada' comes from the Iroquoian word 'kanata.' What does it mean?
expl: Cartier heard two captured guides speak the Iroquoian word kanata, meaning 'village.' By the 1550s, the name of Canada began appearing on maps.

q: Why did the first European explorers call the native peoples of Canada 'Indians'?
opts: The first explorers thought they had reached the East Indies
They spoke languages related to those of India
Early maps labelled North America as India
The word means 'first peoples' in French
expl: Europeans called the native peoples 'Indians' because the first explorers thought they had reached the East Indies.

q: Which Aboriginal group was nomadic, following the bison (buffalo) herd?
expl: The Sioux were nomadic, following the bison (buffalo) herd. The Huron-Wendat were farmers and hunters, the Cree and Dene were hunter-gatherers, and the Inuit lived off Arctic wildlife.

q: Before European contact, West Coast natives preserved fish by drying and smoking.
expl: True. The guide states that West Coast natives preserved fish by drying and smoking.

q: In 1604, the first European settlement north of Florida was established by which French explorers?
opts: Pierre de Monts and Samuel de Champlain
James Wolfe and the Marquis de Montcalm
expl: In 1604, the first European settlement north of Florida was established by French explorers Pierre de Monts and Samuel de Champlain, first on St. Croix Island, then at Port-Royal, in Acadia.

q: In 1608, Samuel de Champlain built a fortress at what is now which city?
expl: In 1608 Champlain built a fortress at what is now Québec City.

q: The vast fur-trade economy of New France was driven by European demand for what?
expl: The French and Aboriginal people collaborated in the vast fur-trade economy, driven by the demand for beaver pelts in Europe.

q: In 1670, King Charles II of England granted exclusive trading rights over the watershed draining into Hudson Bay to which company?
expl: In 1670, King Charles II of England granted the Hudson's Bay Company exclusive trading rights over the watershed draining into Hudson Bay.

q: What did the British victory at the Battle of the Plains of Abraham in 1759 mark?
opts: The start of the American Revolution
The end of France's empire in America
The beginning of the War of 1812
The union of Upper and Lower Canada
expl: In 1759, the British defeated the French in the Battle of the Plains of Abraham at Québec City, marking the end of France's empire in America.

q: Which two commanders were both killed leading their troops at the Battle of the Plains of Abraham?
opts: Brigadier James Wolfe and the Marquis de Montcalm
Sir Isaac Brock and Chief Tecumseh
Count Frontenac and Pierre de Monts
Sir Guy Carleton and Charles de Salaberry
expl: The commanders of both armies, Brigadier James Wolfe and the Marquis de Montcalm, were killed leading their troops in battle in 1759.

q: What did the Quebec Act of 1774 do for the French Roman Catholic majority?
opts: It abolished slavery throughout the British Empire
It divided Quebec into Upper and Lower Canada
It united Upper and Lower Canada as the Province of Canada
It allowed religious freedom for Catholics and permitted them to hold public office
expl: The Quebec Act of 1774 allowed religious freedom for Catholics and permitted them to hold public office, a practice not then allowed in Britain.

q: The Quebec Act of 1774 abolished French civil law in the Province of Quebec.
expl: False. The Quebec Act restored French civil law while maintaining British criminal law.

q: Who were the United Empire Loyalists?
opts: People loyal to the Crown who fled the American Revolution
Fur traders working for the Hudson's Bay Company
Settlers who supported joining the United States
expl: More than 40,000 people loyal to the Crown, called 'Loyalists,' fled the oppression of the American Revolution to settle in Nova Scotia and Quebec.

q: Where was the first representative assembly in what is now Canada elected, in 1758?
expl: The first representative assembly was elected in Halifax, Nova Scotia, in 1758. Prince Edward Island followed in 1773 and New Brunswick in 1785.

q: The Constitutional Act of 1791 divided the Province of Quebec into which two parts?
opts: The Province of Canada and Rupert's Land
expl: The Constitutional Act of 1791 divided the Province of Quebec into Upper Canada (later Ontario), mainly Loyalist, Protestant and English-speaking, and Lower Canada (later Quebec), heavily Catholic and French-speaking.

q: Major-General Sir Isaac Brock captured Detroit in 1812 but was killed while defending against an American attack at which place?
expl: In July 1812 Brock captured Detroit but was killed while defending against an American attack at Queenston Heights, near Niagara Falls, a battle the Americans lost.

q: In 1813, Laura Secord made a dangerous 19-mile (30-km) journey on foot in order to do what?
opts: Deliver dispatches to the Duke of Wellington
Warn Lieutenant James FitzGibbon of a planned American attack
Guide Loyalist refugees to Nova Scotia
expl: In 1813, Laura Secord made a dangerous 19-mile (30-km) journey on foot to warn Lieutenant James FitzGibbon of a planned American attack. Her bravery contributed to victory at the Battle of Beaver Dams.

q: In 1813, who led 460 soldiers, mostly French Canadiens, to turn back 4,000 American invaders at Châteauguay?
expl: In 1813, Lieutenant-Colonel Charles de Salaberry and 460 soldiers, mostly French Canadiens, turned back 4,000 American invaders at Châteauguay, south of Montreal.

q: The War of 1812 helped ensure that Canada would remain independent of the United States.
expl: True. The present-day Canada-U.S.A. border is partly an outcome of the War of 1812, which ensured that Canada would remain independent of the United States.

q: In what year did the British Parliament abolish slavery throughout the Empire?
expl: In 1807 the British Parliament prohibited the buying and selling of slaves, and in 1833 it abolished slavery throughout the Empire.

q: What was the Underground Railroad?
opts: A Christian anti-slavery network that helped slaves escape to Canada
The first railway line between Montreal and Toronto
A secret supply route used during the War of 1812
A trading route of the Hudson's Bay Company
expl: Thousands of slaves escaped from the United States, followed 'the North Star' and settled in Canada via the Underground Railroad, a Christian anti-slavery network.

q: The armed rebellions of 1837-38 had broad public support and succeeded in overthrowing British rule.
expl: False. The rebels did not have enough public support to succeed and were defeated by British troops and Canadian volunteers; a number of rebels were hanged or exiled.

q: After the rebellions of 1837-38, what did Lord Durham recommend?
opts: That Canada join the United States
That the Hudson's Bay Company govern the West
That Quebec become an independent country
That Upper and Lower Canada be merged and given responsible government
expl: Lord Durham, an English reformer sent to report on the rebellions, recommended that Upper and Lower Canada be merged and given responsible government.

q: Under responsible government, what must happen if the government loses a confidence vote in the assembly?
opts: The Governor appoints new ministers from London
A new constitution must be written
expl: Responsible government means the ministers of the Crown must have the support of a majority of the elected representatives; if the government loses a confidence vote in the assembly it must resign. This is the system we have today.

q: Which was the first British North American colony to attain full responsible government, in 1847-48?
expl: The first British North American colony to attain full responsible government was Nova Scotia in 1847-48. In 1848-49 Lord Elgin introduced responsible government in United Canada.

q: Who were the Fathers of Confederation?
opts: The first officers of the North West Mounted Police
The British governors of the Atlantic colonies
Representatives of Nova Scotia, New Brunswick and the Province of Canada
The first judges appointed in the Dominion of Canada
expl: From 1864 to 1867, representatives of Nova Scotia, New Brunswick and the Province of Canada, with British support, worked together to establish a new country. These men are known as the Fathers of Confederation.

q: On what date was the Dominion of Canada officially born?
expl: The British Parliament passed the British North America Act in 1867, and the Dominion of Canada was officially born on July 1, 1867.

q: Which four provinces first formed the Dominion of Canada in 1867?
opts: Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick
Ontario, Quebec, Manitoba and British Columbia
Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland
expl: The old Province of Canada was split into Ontario and Quebec, which, together with New Brunswick and Nova Scotia, formed the new country called the Dominion of Canada in 1867.

q: Until 1982, July 1 was celebrated as 'Dominion Day.'
expl: True. Until 1982, July 1 was celebrated as 'Dominion Day' to commemorate the day Canada became a self-governing Dominion. Today it is officially known as Canada Day.

q: Which Father of Confederation suggested the term 'Dominion of Canada' in 1864, inspired by Psalm 72 in the Bible?
expl: Sir Leonard Tilley, an elected official and Father of Confederation from New Brunswick, suggested the term Dominion of Canada in 1864, inspired by Psalm 72, which refers to 'dominion from sea to sea.'

q: Who became Canada's first Prime Minister in 1867?
expl: In 1867, Sir John Alexander Macdonald, a Father of Confederation, became Canada's first Prime Minister. Born in Scotland on January 11, 1815, his portrait is on the $10 bill.

q: Who was the key architect of Confederation from Quebec?
expl: Sir George-Étienne Cartier was the key architect of Confederation from Quebec. He led Quebec into Confederation and helped negotiate the entry of the Northwest Territories, Manitoba and British Columbia into Canada.

q: Who led the armed uprising of the Métis at Red River and seized Fort Garry in 1869?
expl: When Canada took over the northwest region in 1869, the 12,000 Métis of the Red River were not consulted. In response, Louis Riel led an armed uprising and seized Fort Garry, the territorial capital.

q: What was the outcome for Louis Riel after the second Métis rebellion of 1885 in present-day Saskatchewan?
opts: He negotiated Manitoba's entry into Confederation
He was permanently exiled to the United States
He was tried and executed for high treason
expl: The second rebellion in 1885 led to Riel's trial and execution for high treason, a decision strongly opposed in Quebec. Riel is seen by many as a hero, a defender of Métis rights and the father of Manitoba.

q: Louis Riel was elected to Parliament but never took his seat.
expl: True. After fleeing to the United States in 1870, Riel was elected to Parliament but never took his seat.

q: British Columbia joined Canada in 1871 after Ottawa promised to do what?
opts: Establish a naval base at Victoria
Build a railway to the West Coast
Create the North West Mounted Police
expl: British Columbia joined Canada in 1871 after Ottawa promised to build a railway to the West Coast.

q: Who drove the last spike of the Canadian Pacific Railway on November 7, 1885?
expl: On November 7, 1885, Donald Smith (Lord Strathcona), the Scottish-born director of the Canadian Pacific Railway, drove the last spike, completing a powerful symbol of unity.

q: In what year did Alberta and Saskatchewan become provinces of Canada?
expl: Alberta and Saskatchewan joined Confederation in 1905. Manitoba and the Northwest Territories joined in 1870, British Columbia in 1871, and Yukon Territory in 1898.

q: Which province was the last to join Canada, in 1949?
expl: Newfoundland and Labrador joined Canada in 1949. The territory of Nunavut was later established in 1999.

q: Who became the first French-Canadian prime minister since Confederation and encouraged immigration to the West?
expl: Sir Wilfrid Laurier became the first French-Canadian prime minister since Confederation and encouraged immigration to the West. His portrait is on the $5 bill.

q: About how many Canadians served in the First World War, out of a total population of eight million?
expl: More than 600,000 Canadians served in the First World War, most of them volunteers, out of a total population of eight million. In total, 60,000 Canadians were killed and 170,000 wounded.

q: In April 1917, the Canadian Corps secured its reputation as the 'shock troops of the British Empire' by capturing what?
expl: The Canadian Corps captured Vimy Ridge in April 1917, with 10,000 killed or wounded, securing the Canadians' reputation for valour as the 'shock troops of the British Empire.'

q: Vimy Day is celebrated on November 11.
expl: False. April 9 is celebrated as Vimy Day, marking the capture of Vimy Ridge in 1917. November 11 is Remembrance Day.

q: Who commanded the Canadian Corps in 1918 and is described as Canada's greatest soldier?
expl: In 1918, under the command of General Sir Arthur Currie, Canada's greatest soldier, the Canadian Corps advanced alongside the French and British Empire troops in the last hundred days of the war.

q: The First World War ended with the Armistice on what date?
expl: With Germany and Austria's surrender, the war ended in the Armistice on November 11, 1918. Canadians now observe Remembrance Day each year on November 11.

q: Who composed the poem 'In Flanders Fields' in 1915?
expl: Canadian medical officer Lieutenant-Colonel John McCrae composed the poem 'In Flanders Fields' in 1915; it is often recited on Remembrance Day.

q: Who founded the women's suffrage movement in Canada and was the first Canadian woman to practise medicine in Canada?
expl: The founder of the women's suffrage movement in Canada was Dr. Emily Stowe, the first Canadian woman to practise medicine in Canada.

q: In 1916, which province became the first to grant voting rights to women?
expl: In 1916, Manitoba became the first province to grant voting rights to women. In 1918, most Canadian female citizens aged 21 and over were granted the right to vote in federal elections; Quebec granted women the vote in 1940.

q: Who became the first woman Member of Parliament in 1921?
expl: In 1921 Agnes Macphail, a farmer and teacher, became the first woman MP.

q: Agnes Macphail was the first Canadian woman to practise medicine in Canada.
expl: False. Agnes Macphail was the first woman MP (1921). Dr. Emily Stowe was the first Canadian woman to practise medicine in Canada and founded the suffrage movement.

q: What did the stock market crash of 1929 lead to?
expl: The stock market crash of 1929 led to the Great Depression or the 'Dirty Thirties.' Unemployment reached 27% in 1933 and many businesses were wiped out.

q: Why was the Bank of Canada created in 1934?
opts: To manage the money supply and bring stability to the financial system
To finance the building of the Canadian Pacific Railway
To replace the Montreal Stock Exchange
To pay unemployment insurance during the Depression
expl: The Bank of Canada, a central bank to manage the money supply and bring stability to the financial system, was created in 1934.

q: How did the Second World War begin in 1939?
opts: Germany attacked Belgium and France and Britain declared war
Adolf Hitler, the Nazi dictator of Germany, invaded Poland
expl: The Second World War began in 1939 when Adolf Hitler, the National Socialist (Nazi) dictator of Germany, invaded Poland and conquered much of Europe.

q: On D-Day, June 6, 1944, 15,000 Canadian troops stormed and captured which beach from the German Army?
expl: In the invasion of Normandy on June 6, 1944, known as D-Day, 15,000 Canadian troops stormed and captured Juno Beach from the German Army, a great national achievement.

q: Approximately one in ten Allied soldiers on D-Day was Canadian.
expl: True. Approximately one in ten Allied soldiers on D-Day was Canadian. The Canadian Army went on to liberate the Netherlands in 1944-45.

q: In what year were Aboriginal people granted the right to vote?
expl: Aboriginal people were granted the vote in 1960. In 1948 the Japanese-Canadians, the last Canadians of Asian descent denied the franchise, gained the right to vote.
